threats.title: Lock Duration Bypass via Flawed setLockDuration Math
threats.hypothesis_description: The setLockDuration function contains a critical math flaw where the validation check uses block.timestamp but the actual state assignment uses lastLockTime. This allows users to set their unlockTime to a timestamp in the past, entirely bypassing their lock commitment while keeping rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a user locked tokens significantly in the past, they can pass the reduction check with a small duration, resulting in an unlockTime that is already passed. This breaks the core economic invariant of time-locked rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation check uses block.timestamp + _duration, but assignment uses lastLockTime + _duration

preconditions: User has an existing locked position
Sufficient time has passed since lastLockTime such that block.timestamp + new_duration >= old_unlockTime

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User can set a small _duration that passes the check but results in unlockTime < block.timestamp
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User extracts lockdrop NFTs and schnibbles rewards without honoring the time lock, then immediately withdraws principal

economic_questions: What is the exact mathematical threshold where this bypass becomes executable?
Can this be executed atomically in a single block?
How much reward value can be extracted per bypassed lock?
validation_requirements: Simulate a lockdrop deposit, advance time, and execute setLockDuration to verify immediate unlock capability
Calculate the maximum possible reward extraction vs time committed

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Zero additional capital (uses already locked funds)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Minimal gas for setLockDuration and unlock transactions
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of time-locked rewards (NFTs/schnibbles) without the time risk
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite ROI on the bypassed time value
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block execution once time threshold is reached

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after the mathematical threshold is crossed (when block.timestamp + small_duration >= old_unlockTime)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol TVL drops unexpectedly, reward dilution for honest lockers

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All locked TVL that has passed the mathematical threshold

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the exact inequality conditions in Solidity 0.8.25

threats.title: Indefinite Fund Freezing via lockOnBehalf Griefing
threats.hypothesis_description: The lockOnBehalf function allows any user to lock 0 tokens (or 1 wei) for a victim, which unconditionally resets the victim's unlockTime to the current timestamp plus their full lockDuration, permanently freezing their funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can spend near-zero cost to continuously push a victim's unlock time into the future, acting as a permanent denial of service on their capital.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unlockTime is unconditionally overwritten to block.timestamp + _lockDuration, even for 0 value locks

preconditions: Victim has an active lock with a set lockDuration > 0

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls lockOnBehalf with 0 amount, resetting the unlock timer
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but massive economic damage to victim (griefing/extortion vector)

economic_questions: Can the attacker execute this with exactly 0 ERC20 tokens?
Is there any way for the victim to prevent this extension?
Could this be used for extortion (pay me or I freeze your funds forever)?
validation_requirements: Test lockOnBehalf with 0 amount for standard ERC20s
Verify if victim can front-run the griefing tx (not possible on Blast due to no mempool)

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Extortion value (demanding payment to stop griefing)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Periodic execution (e.g., once every lockDuration period)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before the victim's lock is about to expire
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user trust, permanent lockup of protocol TVL

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check ERC20 transferFrom behavior with 0 amount

threats.title: Permanent Loss of NFT Remainder on Subsequent Locks
threats.hypothesis_description: If a user locks tokens outside of an active lockdrop period, their previously accrued 'remainder' (partial progress towards an NFT) is unconditionally overwritten to 0, destroying their accrued value.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users are economically penalized for interacting with the protocol outside of specific promotional windows, leading to unintended loss of earned assets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: remainder is initialized to 0 and unconditionally saved to storage if lockdrop is inactive

preconditions: User has a non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop
User locks tokens outside of an active lockdrop period

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lockedToken.remainder = remainder (where remainder defaults to 0 if lockdrop inactive)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol usage outside lockdrop destroys state value
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol unfairly burns user's partial NFT entitlement

economic_questions: What is the average economic value of a remainder?
Are users warned about this state destruction?
validation_requirements: Simulate lockdrop deposit, end lockdrop, and normal deposit to verify remainder zeroing

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: User frustration and loss of partial rewards

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to (nftCost - 1) tokens worth of NFT progress

threats.title: Immediate Unlock Yield Farming via Zero Lock Duration
threats.hypothesis_description: If the protocol's minLockDuration is 0, users can lock and immediately unlock in the same block, potentially triggering reward accrual (schnibbles) without taking on any time-lock risk.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unlock check uses strictly greater than (>) rather than greater than or equal to (>=) for block.timestamp, allowing immediate withdrawal if duration is 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (lockedToken.unlockTime > uint32(block.timestamp)) revert; allows unlockTime == block.timestamp

preconditions: lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 or no lockdrop is active

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0; unlock check passes if unlockTime == block.timestamp
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extracts yield/points generated by forceHarvest without capital lockup

economic_questions: Does forceHarvest grant rewards for zero-duration locks?
Can this be looped in a single transaction via a smart contract?
validation_requirements: Test atomic lock/unlock sequence and measure schnibbles balance change

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on external AccountManager reward logic

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilution of rewards for genuine lockers

threats.title: Protocol Insolvency due to Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract credits users with the full `_quantity` requested during a lock, rather than the actual amount received. If a fee-on-transfer token is configured, the contract becomes insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly states the contract is expected to handle fee-on-transfer tokens, but the code lacks the necessary before/after balance checks, leading to guaranteed insolvency if such a token is deployed.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures a fee-on-transfer token as supported by project documentation
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly states 'The LockManager is expected to handle non-standard ERC20 behaviors, including fee-on-transfer'

proof_of_signal.pattern: lockedToken.quantity += _quantity instead of measuring actual balance change from transferFrom

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured and active

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Users lock and unlock, draining the contract's actual balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early withdrawers get full amounts, last withdrawers lose their principal entirely

economic_questions: Will the protocol actually deploy a FoT token?
How quickly does the insolvency scale with the fee percentage?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock FoT token, configure it, and simulate multiple users locking and unlocking

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Attacker doesn't profit directly, but avoids the loss that late withdrawers suffer
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Over the lifetime of the token's configuration

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of funds for a portion of the user base

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of principal for the last users
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total fee percentage * Total Volume Transacted

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify lack of SafeERC20 and balance checks

threats.title: USD Price Feed Distortion via Single Price Array Application
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function takes an array of token contracts but only a single USD price. When executed, this single price is applied to all tokens in the array, potentially causing massive mispricing if tokens with different values are bundled.
threats.investigation_rationale: If operators attempt to update WETH and USDC in the same proposal, one will be drastically mispriced, distorting the getLockedWeightedValue calculation which drives protocol rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: A single proposedPrice is applied in a loop to all tokenContract addresses in the proposal array

preconditions: Price feed operators propose a price update for multiple tokens with different actual USD values in a single transaction

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator error or malicious proposal bundles high-value and low-value tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users holding the artificially inflated token receive vastly disproportionate rewards

economic_questions: How does getLockedWeightedValue impact the downstream schnibbles emission?
Can operators be tricked into bundling tokens?
validation_requirements: Simulate a bundled proposal and measure the impact on getLockedWeightedValue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Existing locked positions in the mispriced token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: None for the user (relies on operator action)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Thousands of times normal reward rate (e.g., if USDC is priced at WETH value)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until a new price proposal corrects it

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe distortion of protocol economy and unfair reward distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Holders of the correctly priced or undervalued tokens

threats.title: Multi-Sig Vote Inflation via Disapprove-then-Approve Loophole
threats.hypothesis_description: A price feed operator can cast both a disapproval and an approval for the same proposal, inflating both counters. This breaks the mutual exclusivity of voting and can be used to manipulate proposal execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because approveUSDPrice does not check if the operator has already disapproved, an operator can artificially inflate the disapprovalsCount to block a legitimate proposal, or manipulate the threshold logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: disapproveUSDPrice sets disapprovals mapping, but approveUSDPrice only checks approvals mapping, allowing double voting

preconditions: A USD price update proposal is active
An operator wants to manipulate the approval/disapproval counts

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Operator increments both disapprovalsCount and approvalsCount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Allows a minority of operators to block legitimate price updates or force stale prices, which can be traded against

economic_questions: Can this be used to permanently halt price updates?
What is the economic value of keeping a stale price active?
validation_requirements: Simulate the voting sequence to verify both counters increment for the same sender

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on the value of trading against a stale oracle price

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When market prices diverge significantly from the stale protocol price
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol uses incorrect USD values for weighted calculations
